---
title: "Tropical Mexican Clover (Richardia brasiliensis)"
slug: "richardia-brasiliensis"
group: "Plantae"
image: "https://inaturalist-open-data.s3.amazonaws.com/photos/354584166/medium.jpg"        # keep for thumbnails
image-alt: "Tropical Mexican Clover"  # keep for a11y
tax_kingdom: "Plantae"
tax_class: "Magnoliopsida"
tax_order: "Gentianales"
tax_family: "Rubiaceae"
categories: ["Plantae", "Magnoliopsida", "Gentianales", "Rubiaceae"]   # these drive the chips + filtering
freeze: true
---

<figure class="species-figure">

<img src="https://inaturalist-open-data.s3.amazonaws.com/photos/354584166/medium.jpg" alt="Tropical Mexican Clover" class="species-photo"/>

<figcaption class="photo-credit">

Photo: <a href="https://www.inaturalist.org/people/robmcirving">
robmcirving</a> (CC BY-NC) via <a href="https://www.inaturalist.org/observations/200923813">iNaturalist</a>

</figcaption>

</figure>

## Taxonomy

-   **Class:** Magnoliopsida
-   **Order:** Gentianales
-   **Family:** Rubiaceae

## Occurrence at DeLuca Preserve

-   [DeLuca Bio Blitz Species Observations](https://www.inaturalist.org/observations?project_id=191727&subview=map&taxon_id=167940)

## Conservation & rarity context

-   IUCN: Not Listed
-   **Rarity note:** Rare species in Florida found in lawns, roadsides, and other disturbed areas.

## Natural history notes

A low-growing herb characterized by its opposite leaves and small white, star-shaped flowers clustered at the leaf nodes. In Florida, it is commonly found in lawns, roadsides, and other disturbed areas, often in sandy or compact soils. It grows during the warmer months and can spread to form patches, frequently behaving as a common lawn weed.

## References / links

-   [iNaturalist species page](https://www.inaturalist.org/taxa/167940-Richardia-brasiliensis)
-   [Local profile](https://mrec.ifas.ufl.edu/resources/weedsbyflowercolor/white/richardiabrasiliensis/)
